name: Stanley Turrentine
description: American jazz saxophonist and record producer (1934–2000)
abstract: Stanley William Turrentine (April 5, 1934 – September 12, 2000), nicknamed Mr. T, was an American Grammy nominated jazz tenor saxophonist and record producer.

about_quotes.speaker: Hubert Laws
about_quotes.evidence: There's another record we did with Stanley Turrentine, just – in fact, it was released after he died. This is a record I did with Stanley in New York at some – I can’t even remember the studio.
about_quotes.doc_title: Hubert Laws — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Kenny Burrell
about_quotes.evidence: I had been working with, by that time, with Stanley Turrentine, you know we did all these records with Jimmy Smith and all these other people.
about_quotes.doc_title: Kenny Burrell — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Mark Soskin
about_quotes.evidence: I played a lot with Herbie and yeah, at one time I was playing with Herbie, Stanley Turrentine and Sonny at the same time.
about_quotes.doc_title: Mark Soskin interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, September 17, 2021

about_quotes.speaker: George Benson
about_quotes.evidence: Stanley Turrentine’s record Sugar was a fantastic record, with me and Freddie Hubbard.
about_quotes.doc_title: George Benson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Teddy McRae
about_quotes.evidence: He did for me on Blue Note "Broadway" on two or three different records.

about_quotes.speaker: Harold Mabern
about_quotes.evidence: Now you’ve got Louis Jordan and the Tympany Five. George Coleman and Jimmy Anastos played with B.B. King’s blues band, rhythm and blues. George Coleman took the first alto solo on “Woke Up This Morning” (my baby’s gone), that was a hit, you see, so we all came up through the blues. No when I listen
about_quotes.doc_title: Harold Mabern interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, March 31, 2010
